channel.title: Chris KY FUNG's Blog
channel.description: Feel free to bookmark this to keep an eye on my project updates

item.title: Instaparser: Leveraging a Decade of Instapaper Tech for Rock-Solid Content Extraction
item.description:
In the era of Generative AI, the “Garbage In, Garbage Out” (GIGO) principle has never been more relevant. For developers building RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines or AI agents, the biggest bottleneck isn’t the LLM—it’s the data. Web scraping is easy; maintaining scrapers across thousands of shifting CSS selectors, hidden sidebars, and cluttered DOM structures is a nightmare.

If you’re tired of your automation flows breaking every time a target site tweaks its layout, it’s time to offload the heavy lifting to a professional engine. Enter Instaparser: a specialized extraction service designed to let you focus on your core logic while it handles the “messy” web.

TL;DR:

  • The Pedigree: Built on the same battle-tested engine that has powered Instapaper for over a decade.
  • Core APIs: High-fidelity Article, PDF, and Summary APIs that convert messy URLs into clean, structured JSON.
  • Developer Experience: Native Python/Node.js SDKs and first-class n8n integration.
  • Transparent Economics: A credit-based system with 1,000 free monthly credits; paid tiers start at $150/mo with predictable scaling.
  • Market Fit: While competitors focus on broad “crawling,” Instaparser specializes in high-accuracy “content parsing”—making it the most stable choice for high-quality data pipelines.

What exactly is Instaparser?

You might not know Instaparser yet, but you definitely know Instapaper. As one of the original pioneers of the “Read-it-Later” category, Instapaper has spent 18 years perfecting the art of stripping away the noise from the web.

Instaparser is that core technology—refined over millions of pages and countless CMS variations—packaged as a commercial API. Launched as a standalone service in March 2026, it offers three production-ready endpoints:

  • Article API: Extracts titles, authors, clean body text, and metadata from any URL with ~95% accuracy across all web layouts.
  • PDF API: Parses PDFs (via URL or upload) into structured text or clean HTML.
  • Summary API: Uses intelligent extraction to pull key sentences, ideal for summarizing long-form content before feeding it into your LLM.

Quick Start

Instaparser’s onboarding is refreshingly lean. You can sign-up your account and test the service without ever touching a credit card:

  1. Register: Head to instaparser.com and hit “Get API Key.”
  2. Verify: Fill in your details and verify your email.
  3. Deploy: Your unique API Key is sent straight to your inbox.
  4. Explore: Log in to your dashboard to monitor usage and manage your environment.
  5. Free Tier: Every user gets 1,000 free credits monthly to stress-test the API.

Pricing & Unit Economics

Instaparser uses a “pay-for-success” credit system. You are only billed for successful API calls, making your burn rate highly predictable.

Tier Monthly Fee Included Credits Overage (per credit) Rate Limit
Trial Free 1,000 N/A 1 req/sec
Beta $150 100,000 $0.0012 5 req/sec
Live $500 500,000 $0.0011 25 req/sec
Scale $900 1,000,000 $0.001 50 req/sec

Real-World Unit Costs (Beta Tier):

  • Extracting 1 Web Article = 1 Credit ≈ $0.0015
  • Generating 1 Summary = 10 Credits ≈ $0.015
  • Parsing a 10-page PDF = 10 × 5 Credits ≈ $0.075

Competitor Matrix: Where does Instaparser fit?

  1. Firecrawl: Designed specifically for RAG with Markdown output. While it offers a great developer experience, its unit costs are significantly higher (e.g., ~$125/mo for only 3,000 crawls), especially when dynamic rendering is involved.
  2. Bright Data: The enterprise heavyweight. They specialize in “getting the HTML” through massive proxy networks to beat anti-bot measures, but the cleaning and parsing logic is still on you.
  3. Crawl4AI: The open-source darling. It’s “free” if your time is free, but you’ll bear the infrastructure costs and the constant maintenance required to dodge IP bans.
Tool Avg. Unit Cost Anti-Bot Depth Ease of Use
Instaparser ~$0.0015 / page Moderate High
Firecrawl ~$0.0417 / crawl Moderate Medium
Bright Data ~$0.0015 / request High Low
Crawl4AI Server Costs Only Low Low

Implementation: From n8n to Python SDK

1. Native n8n Integration

For the No-Code and Low-Code crowd, the official n8n-nodes-instaparser community node is a game changer. Just plug in your API key, and you can pipe clean JSON directly into Notion, Airtable, or your vector database.

2. Lightweight SDKs

If you’re building custom apps, Instaparser’s SDKs are among the leanest I’ve seen.

  • Python: pip install instaparser
  • Node.js: npm install instaparser-api

The logic is unified across both languages—whether you’re handling a complex URL or a messy PDF, the code remains minimal and readable.

Final Thoughts: Why Instaparser is Worth Considering

As an AI engineer, I see three reasons why Instaparser is becoming a sleeper hit in the dev community:

1. Technical Legacy as a Moat

In an industry full of “wrapper” startups, Instaparser has a genuine pedigree. Handling the “long tail” of weird edge cases in web formatting is something you can only learn by doing it for a decade. This translates to fewer “broken” parses for your application.

2. The LLM Noise Filter

LLMs are expensive and have finite context windows. Feeding them raw HTML is a waste of tokens. Instaparser acts as a high-fidelity “pre-processor,” ensuring that your AI is only looking at relevant content, which improves both accuracy and cost-efficiency.

3. Operational Stability

Most teams underestimate the “maintenance tax” of web scrapers. By using a managed parsing service, you trade a fraction of a cent per call for hours of saved engineering time.

Conclusion

Stop debugging scrapers at 2 AM. Leverage ten years of Instapaper engineering to feed your AI agents the cleanest data possible.

Unlike Firecrawl or Crawl4AI, which focus on the quantity of pages crawled, Instaparser focuses on the quality of the parse. For teams that prioritize uptime and low maintenance, it is the most friction-less way to turn the chaotic web into a reliable database.

If you’re building a market research tool, a personal knowledge base, or an autonomous AI agent, Instaparser is the missing piece of your stack. With 1,000 free credits a month, there’s no reason not to give it a spin. In automation, stability is the ultimate feature—and Instaparser delivers it in spades.

item.title: Automate Box File Renaming with n8n: The Complete Workflow Guide
item.description:
Reclaim Your Time from Manual File Management

Have you ever found yourself spending an afternoon just renaming files in Box? Maybe you’re preparing a client deliverable, organizing quarterly reports, or cleaning up a messy shared drive. You click, type, hit enter, repeat. By the 50th file, your wrist hurts, and your mind wanders.

I feel you. I’ve seen brilliant teams lose hours of creative energy to repetitive admin work. It doesn’t have to be this way.

Today, I’m walking you through building a n8n workflow that renames Box files automatically. Whether you’re standardizing naming conventions or batching updates, this setup will handle the heavy lifting while you focus on what matters.

Let’s build something that works for you.

TL;DR

  • Goal: Automatically rename files stored in Box cloud storage.
  • Tool: n8n (workflow automation platform).
  • Core Nodes: Manual Trigger → Box Search → Filter → HTTP Request (PUT).
  • Key Config: Use Folder ID 0 for root search; employ Box API PUT endpoint for renaming.
  • Time Saved: Hours per week on manual data entry.
  • Difficulty: Beginner to Intermediate.

🗺️ The Workflow Architecture

Before we dive into the nodes, let’s visualize the journey. Our workflow is a linear pipeline designed for safety and precision. We don’t just rename everything; we search, verify, then act.

This structure ensures we only touch the files we intend to change.

🛠️ Step 1: The Manual Trigger

Every great workflow starts with control. While we can automate this to run on a schedule later, starting with a Manual Trigger gives you the confidence to test without unintended consequences.

  • Node: Manual Trigger
  • Why: Allows you to run the workflow on demand during setup.
  • Tip: Once perfected, swap this for a Schedule Trigger to run nightly.

📌 Reminder: Always manual testing first. Ensure you see exactly what data is moving before it goes live.

🔍 Step 2: Search Files in Box

Now, we need to find the files that need renaming. The Box node in n8n is powerful, but it needs clear direction.

Configuration Details

  • Resource: File
  • Operation: Search
  • Folder IDs: Enter 0 to search from the Root Folder.
  • Query: The string to search for (matches names, descriptions, content).

Leveraging Advanced Search Filters

Beyond the basic query and folder IDs, the Box Search node offers robust filtering capabilities to refine your search. These options are crucial for targeting specific file types, statuses, or timeframes, ensuring precision in your automation.

  • File Extensions: Target files by their extension (e.g., pdf, docx, xlsx). This is particularly useful when you need to process only documents of a certain format.
  • Sort: Define the order in which search results are returned (either Relevance or Modified At). Sorting can simplify subsequent processing steps.
  • Trash Content: Include or exclude items that are in the trash. This can be set to Trashed Only to search only within the trash, or Non Trashed Only to exclude trashed items from the search results.
  • Content Type: Filter by the type of content (e.g., document, image, video). This can help narrow down searches in repositories with diverse file types.
  • Created At Range: Specify a date range for when files were created. Ideal for processing recently added files or historical data.
  • Size Range: Filter files based on their size. File size ranges are defined as comma-separated byte sizes (e.g., 1000,2000 for files between 1KB and 2KB). Useful for identifying large documents or excluding small, irrelevant files.
  • Updated At Range: Define a date range for when files were last modified. This is effective for workflows that need to react to recent changes or synchronize data.

These advanced filters empower you to create highly specific and efficient search queries, minimizing the risk of unintended operations on your Box files.

Why This Matters: The query string is your filter’s first line of defense. It matches against item names, descriptions, and even text content within files. Be specific here to avoid pulling in unrelated documents.

🚥 Step 3: The Filter Node (Your Safety Net)

Search queries can sometimes be too broad. That’s where the Filter Node shines. It acts as a gatekeeper, ensuring only files meeting your exact criteria proceed to the renaming stage.

Common Filter Logic

You might want to rename only files named with certain prefix, or only files created after a specific date.

📡 Step 4: HTTP Request for Renaming

This is where the magic happens. Since the n8n Box node currently does not offer a direct file renaming operation, using the HTTP Request node is necessary to access the Box API for this specific action.

Configuration

  • Method: PUT
  • URL: https://api.box.com/2.0/files/{{ $json.id }}
  • Credential Type: Box OAuth2 API
  • Send Body: JSON

  • Body Content:

      {
    "name": "New_File_Name.ext"
  }

💡 Pro Tips: Dynamic Naming

You don’t have to hardcode names. Use expressions to transform existing data:

{
  "name": "{{ $json.name.replace('OLD_PATTERN', 'NEW_STRING') }}"
}

This expression uses JavaScript’s replace() method to transform the existing filename ($json.name). You would replace 'OLD_PATTERN' with the specific text or regular expression you want to find, and 'NEW_STRING' with the desired replacement. For instance, to replace “Draft” with “Final” in a filename, the expression would be {{ $json.name.replace('Draft', 'Final') }}.

🚀 Implementation Checklist

Ready to build? Here’s your quick launch plan:

  1. Credentials: Ensure your Box OAuth2 credentials are active in n8n.
  2. Build: Drag and drop the four nodes as described.
  3. Test: Run manually with a single test file in Box.
  4. Verify: Check Box to confirm the rename occurred.
  5. Activate: Schedule or deploy for team use.

⚠️ Security & Privacy Note

Please handle API credentials with care. Never share your tokens publicly. Use n8n’s built-in credential management to keep your secrets secure. Protecting your digital identity is just as important as automating your tasks.

🔧 Troubleshooting Common Hurdles

Even the best workflows hit bumps. Here’s how to smooth them out:

Issue Likely Cause Solution
Auth Error Invalid Token Reconnect Box credentials in n8n settings.
No Files Found Query too specific Broaden your search string or check Folder ID.
Wrong Files Renamed Filter missing Add stricter conditions in the Filter node.
Rate Limit Too many requests Add batch interval in the HTTP Request node.

🌱 Taking It Further

Once you’ve mastered this basic renaming workflow, the world is your oyster. Consider:

  • Logging: Send a summary to Slack or Google Sheets.
  • Versioning: Move original files to an archive folder before renaming.
  • Conditional Logic: Apply different naming rules based on file type.

Automation is a journey, not a destination. Each workflow you build teaches you something new about efficiency and care.

📢 Join the Conversation

Have you built a similar workflow? I’d love to hear about your experience. Did you encounter any unique challenges with the Box API?

👇 Drop a comment below or share this guide with a colleague who’s drowning in manual file tasks. Let’s build a community of efficient, empowered creators.

Download the Workflow Template

item.title: Declutter Instapaper for Free with Carrot2: A No-Code PoC Worflow
item.description:
The Challenge: Too Many Bookmarks, No System

You’ve been using Instapaper for months or even years. Articles about various subjects, for example, career, personal development, productivity tips, programming tutorials, and random news stories—all sitting in one giant “Articles” folder. When you want to dive deep into a topic, you’re scrolling through hundreds of unrelated links.

Sound familiar?

The problem isn’t that Instapaper is bad. It’s that manually organizing 300+ articles into folders and tags takes forever. What if you could just upload your bookmarks and let a smart tool automatically group them into topics?

That’s where Carrot2 comes in.

Carrot2 is a brilliant open-source tool that transforms your messy document collection into organized, thematic clusters. And the best part? No coding, no complex machine learning knowledge required. Just upload your data, click a button, and watch as it uncovers the hidden themes in your reading list. Think of it as an intelligent librarian for your digital brain.

In this tutorial, I’ll show you how to use Carrot2 with your Instapaper exports to create a visual map of your reading interests.

Skip table of contents
Table of Contents
  • The Challenge: Too Many Bookmarks, No System
  • What is Carrot2?
  • Why This Will Revolutionize Your Reading List
  • Part 1: Prepare Your Data
  • Part 2: Getting Started with Carrot2
    • Option A: The Online Demo (Fastest Route)
    • Option B: Local Installation (Maximum Privacy)
  • Part 3: Let the Clustering Begin!
  • Part 4: Explore Your Results
  • Part 5: Choosing the Right Algorithm
  • Part 6: Refine and Export
    • Interpreting Weak Results
    • Export Your Results
  • Carrot2 vs. The Old Way: No Contest
  • Things to Keep in Mind
  • Troubleshooting: When Results Seem Wrong
  • Your Next Move
  • Final Thoughts
  • Resources

What is Carrot2?

In simple terms, Carrot2 is a “clustering engine.” Here’s what that means for you:

You feed it a collection of texts—like your Instapaper articles. Carrot2 reads through them all, identifies similarities, and automatically groups them into meaningful categories. It then labels these groups with short, descriptive phrases and presents them in stunning, interactive visualizations. You can explore your clusters, drill down into specific topics, and finally understand the true shape of your knowledge.

Unlike traditional folder-based organization (where you manually decide categories), Carrot2 discovers patterns in your data. It’s fast, runs in your web browser, and requires zero programming knowledge.

Why it’s a game-changer:

  • Completely free and open-source (no ads, no paywalls)
  • Works entirely in your browser (no scary command line)
  • Designed by researchers specifically for organizing documents
  • Can run locally on your computer (your data never leaves your machine)
  • Works perfectly with Instapaper export data

Why This Will Revolutionize Your Reading List

When you apply this clustering magic to your Instapaper library, you gain:

  • Quick overview: See at a glance what topics dominate your reading habits
  • Efficient browsing: Find related articles on a subject without endless scrolling
  • Pattern discovery: Realize “Oh, I really do read a lot about productivity and AI”
  • Smarter organization: Use the discovered clusters to better organize your bookmarks in the future
  • Time saved: Stop manually categorizing—let the algorithm do the work

Part 1: Prepare Your Data

First things first, you need to get your bookmarks out of Instapaper. If you haven’t already, the Instapaper Scraper tool is your best friend.

Install it with a simple command:

pip install instapaper-scraper

Then, export your articles into a CSV file:

instapaper-scraper --format csv --output instapaper_bookmarks.csv

Open your CSV file in Excel, Google Sheets, or any spreadsheet application. You should see columns like:

id title url
123… Introduction to Neural Networks https://…
456… React Hooks Tutorial https://…
789… Notion for Second Brains https://…

You can also export to JSON, which is a better option for international users:

instapaper-scraper --format json --output instapaper_bookmarks.json

Either way, you end up with a data file packed with your bookmarks. This is the fuel for Carrot2.

Important note: If your bookmark titles include non-Latin characters (like CJK or Greek), you should definitely use the JSON format. As of version 4.8.3, Carrot2 has a small bug with reading these characters from CSV files.

Part 2: Getting Started with Carrot2

You have two paths here—the quick and easy way, or the super-private way.

Option A: The Online Demo (Fastest Route)

Visit the Carrot2 Clustering Workbench at the official demo site:

🔗 https://search.carrot2.org/#/workbench

This is the quickest way to test the waters without installing a single thing. Just visit the site in any modern browser.

A quick note on privacy: The online demo has a limitation—your data is temporarily transferred to the Carrot2 server for processing. While nothing is stored permanently, if you’re concerned about privacy, you might prefer the local option.

Option B: Local Installation (Maximum Privacy)

If you’d rather keep your reading data completely on your own machine, this is the way to go.

What you’ll need:

  • Java 11 or a newer version (it’s free and available from java.com).

Installation steps:

  1. Download the latest Carrot2 release from their GitHub page.
  2. Extract the downloaded ZIP file to a folder on your computer.
  3. Navigate into the dcs folder and run the appropriate script: dcs for Mac/Linux or dcs.cmd for Windows.
  4. Open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8080.

That’s it. Carrot2 is now running on your machine, and all your data stays private.

Pro-tip: If you want to change the port number, you can use the --port command-line parameter. For example:

dcs --port 1234

Part 3: Let the Clustering Begin!

Whether you’re online or local, the interface is identical. Here’s what to do:

Step 1: Choose Your Data Source

  • In the “Data source” dropdown at the top left, select “Local file”.

Step 2: Upload Your Bookmarks

  • Click the upload area and select your instapaper_bookmarks.csv (or .json) file.
  • You can also just drag and drop the file onto the page.

Step 3: Choose Fields to Cluster

  • The tool will automatically detect the text fields in your file.
  • For best results, make sure “title” and “description” (if you have it) are checked.
  • Uncheck nonverbal fields like “id” and “url” to avoid confusing the algorithm.

Step 4: Click “Cluster”

  • The software analyzes your data and generates clusters

This usually takes just a few seconds for collections under 1,000 bookmarks. Sit back and let the magic happen.

Part 4: Explore Your Results

Once the analysis is done, you’ll see your bookmarks neatly sorted into groups. Each cluster comes with:

  • A descriptive label (e.g., “Python,” “Machine Learning,” “Data Science”)
  • The article count for that group
  • A list of the articles within that cluster

Carrot2 offers a few fantastic ways to visualize this data:

  1. List View (For the Details) This is your home base. It shows a simple list of clusters. Click on any cluster to see the articles inside. It’s perfect for carefully exploring what the algorithm has uncovered.

  2. Treemap View (For the Big Picture) This view turns your clusters into a mosaic of rectangles. The bigger the rectangle, the more bookmarks it contains. At a glance, you can see which topics dominate your reading. Is “Python” a giant slab and “Machine Learning” a tiny sliver? Now you know.

  3. Pie Chart View (For a Quick Snapshot) A clean, simple pie chart that shows the percentage of your library dedicated to each cluster. It’s a great way to quickly grasp the overall shape of your interests.

Switch between these views using the tabs at the top of the results panel.

Part 5: Choosing the Right Algorithm

Behind the scenes, Carrot2 uses different mathematical approaches to clustering. You don’t need to understand the technical details, but here’s what you need to know:

Algorithm Best For Speed Label Quality
Lingo High-quality, descriptive labels Medium ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
STC Very fast clustering, large collections Very Fast ⭐⭐⭐
K-Means Simple, non-overlapping clusters Very Fast ⭐⭐

For Instapaper bookmarks, Lingo is your best bet. It produces the most descriptive labels, making it easy to see what each group is about at a glance.

To switch algorithms, just use the “Algorithm” dropdown on the left, make your selection, and hit “Cluster” again.

Part 6: Refine and Export

Interpreting Weak Results

If your clusters feel a little off—maybe the labels are vague or articles seem mismatched—here are a few quick fixes:

  1. Mixed-language content? Specify your primary language in the language dropdown to improve accuracy.
  2. Clusters too broad? The STC algorithm can sometimes create more specific, fine-grained groups.
  3. Not enough clusters? While the defaults are usually great, you can always experiment with the clustering parameters in the left-side panel.

Export Your Results

Once you’re happy with the clusters, it’s time to put them to work.

  1. Click the Export button in the top right of the results panel.
  2. Choose your preferred format:
    • Excel (super easy to work with)
    • CSV (great for Google Sheets)
    • JSON (for more advanced uses)

The export includes:

  • NEW: A “Cluster” column showing which group each article belongs to
  • All your original columns (id, title, url, etc.)

Now you have a spreadsheet that looks like this:

Cluster id title url
Machine Learning 123… Introduction to Neural Networks https://…
Web Development 456… React Hooks Tutorial https://…
Productivity 789… Notion for Second Brains https://…

So, what can you do with this?

  • Build a better second brain: Import it into Notion or Obsidian and organize your notes by cluster.
  • Create a reading roadmap: “This week, I’m tackling the ‘Machine Learning’ cluster.”
  • Share curated reading lists: Easily send topic-specific article lists to friends or colleagues.
  • Identify knowledge gaps: Are there topics you thought you read more about? Now you’ll know.

Carrot2 vs. The Old Way: No Contest

Task Manual (Folders & Tags) Carrot2
Time to organize 300 articles 2-3 grueling hours ~5 minutes
Discover unexpected themes Unlikely Almost guaranteed
Re-organize with new logic Another weekend gone 30 seconds (re-cluster)
Visualize your knowledge Impossible Beautiful & interactive
Keep data private Yes Yes (with local install)
Learn new topics All on you Carrot2 guides the way

Things to Keep in Mind

Be aware of these practical limits:

  • Best for 100–1,000 bookmarks: Carrot2 shines with small-to-medium collections. With 10,000+ articles, it will be slower and more memory-intensive.
  • The Lingo algorithm can be slower: If you’re clustering a massive collection and things feel sluggish, switch to the faster STC algorithm.
  • Garbage in, garbage out: Bookmarks with vague or short titles won’t cluster as effectively as those with rich, descriptive ones.

Troubleshooting: When Results Seem Wrong

  • “One cluster is huge, others are tiny” → Your bookmarks might naturally cluster that way. Or try a different algorithm (STC instead of Lingo).

  • “Articles in the same cluster don’t seem related” → Maybe they share some keywords but aren’t semantically related. Try re-clustering with different parameters or check if those articles have messy/generic titles.

  • “Carrot2 won’t upload my file!” → Double-check that your file is a valid CSV or JSON. When in doubt, the online demo is a great fallback.

  • “Results are too granular (100 tiny clusters)” → Try the Lingo algorithm and adjust the parameter panel. Some algorithms create more clusters by default.

  • “I want to change the cluster labels” → You can’t directly edit cluster labels in Carrot2, but you can export the results and manually rename clusters in Excel if needed.

Your Next Move

Once you’ve discovered your clusters, here’s what you can do:

  1. Reorganize Instapaper: Create folders based on cluster labels and move bookmarks accordingly
  2. Set reading goals: Pick a cluster you want to focus on this week (e.g., “Machine Learning”) and work through those articles
  3. Share your patterns: The cluster breakdown tells you a lot about your interests—it can inform what you read next
  4. Combine with other tools: Use the cluster information to feed your personal knowledge management system (Obsidian, Notion, Google NotebookLM)

Final Thoughts

Carrot2 does more than just sort a list; it transforms a flat, overwhelming backlog of bookmarks into a vibrant, discoverable landscape of knowledge. By automating the soul-crushing work of categorization, it frees you up to do what you intended all along: actually read, learn, and grow.

The best part? It’s completely free, surprisingly fast, and works right in your browser. No advanced technical skills required—just your curiosity and your reading list.

Have you tried clustering your own bookmarks? I’d love to hear about it. Drop a comment below and share the most interesting patterns you discovered in your reading habits!

Resources

  • Carrot2 Official Site
  • Carrot2 Online Demo
  • Carrot2 GitHub Repository
  • Java Downloads (if installing locally)
  • Carrot2 - Wikipedia
  • Carrot2 Clustering Workbench Documentation
  • Algorithm Comparison
  • Lingo3G or Carrot2? | Carrot Search
  • Carrot2 Performance Guidelines
  • Personalized Library Book Recommendations Using K-Means Clustering and Association Rules – Journal of Scientometric Research
  • Clustering Snippets With Carrot2 - Index Data
  • Analysis and Comparison of Web Document Clustering Algorithms with Lingo – IJERT

item.title: InstapaperScraper v1.1: Now with JSON, SQLite Exports, and a Better CLI
item.description:
It’s time for a big update! I’m excited to announce the release of InstapaperScraper v1.1, a significant upgrade that makes the tool more powerful, flexible, and easier to use.

The original version was a simple script to help users export their bookmarks to CSV. This new version builds on that foundation, adding more export options and a much-improved user experience, while remaining a reliable tool for those who prefer not to use an API.

Let’s dive into what’s new.

Skip table of contents
Table of Contents
  • What’s New in v1.1?
  • Getting Started with v1.1
  • Your Data, Your Rules
  • Contribute & Stay Updated

What’s New in v1.1?

This release focuses on flexibility, robustness, and a more modern developer experience.

  • Multiple Export Formats: You asked for it, you got it! In addition to CSV, you can now export your bookmarks to JSON and SQLite. This gives you the power to use your data in a wide range of applications, from data analysis pipelines to custom web apps.

  • TOML for Configuration: We’ve moved away from .env files to config.toml. This provides a standardized, more expressive way to configure folder settings and other options.

  • Powerful CLI: The script now features a command-line interface (CLI) powered by argparse. You can specify folders, output and provides clear options to select export formats and manage authentication.

Getting Started with v1.1

Getting started is simple. The tool is now a proper Python package available on PyPI.

  1. Install the Package:

     pip install instapaper-scraper

  2. Configure (Optional): To scrape specific folders, create a config.toml file. You can place it in your user config directory (~/.config/instapaper-scraper/config.toml) or the working directory.

     # Example config.toml
 [[folders]]
 key = "ml"
 id = "1234567"
 slug = "machine-learning"
 output_filename = "ml-articles.json"

  3. Run the Scraper: The script will securely prompt for your credentials on the first run and save your session.

     # Export to the default CSV format
 instapaper-scraper

 # Export to JSON
 instapaper-scraper --format json

 # Export a specific folder to a custom SQLite database
 instapaper-scraper --folder ml --format sqlite --output my_ml_articles.db

    You can also pass your credentials directly via the command line if you prefer.

Your Data, Your Rules

With these new export options, you have more control than ever:

  • Analyze with SQLite: Run complex SQL queries on your bookmarks to uncover reading patterns.
  • Integrate with JSON: Easily feed your data into other applications or web services.
  • Keep it Simple with CSV: Stick with the classic format for quick analysis in any spreadsheet program.

Contribute & Stay Updated

InstapaperScraper is an open-source project. If you find it useful, please consider starring it on GitHub! Your contributions are also welcome—feel free to report issues, suggest features, or submit pull requests.

Happy reading, and may your data bring you as much joy as the articles you’ve saved!

item.title: Get a FREE, Automated YouTube Subscriber Tracker in Google Sheets
item.description:
TL;DR:

  • What it is: A simple, open-source script for Google Sheets (Google Apps Script).
  • What it does: Automatically pulls new subscriber names and their channel links directly from your YouTube notification emails.
  • The Best Part: It archives the data in a sheet AND moves the processed emails to the trash, decluttering your Gmail inbox while you sleep.
  • The Outcome: A permanent, one-click engagement list for building loyal fans.

Why You Need This Free Script

For New YouTubers & Growing Channels:

We’ve all been there: The dopamine hit from a ”[New User] has subscribed to you on YouTube!” email. But that feeling fades instantly as the email gets buried in your inbox.

If you are just checking YouTube Studio for your total count, you are treating your audience like a metric. But for a growing channel, your first followers are the people you need to impress, engage with, and thank.

Your first 1,000 subscribers are your founding members. They deserve a personalized thank-you. Stop relying on fleeting email notifications and start building a real community roster.

This is where the youtube-subscriber-spreadsheet-apps-script helps you build a real connection and a permanent record of your channel’s history—all for free.

The Bootstrap Method: How We Get the Data for Free

Instead of wrestling with complex YouTube APIs, this project uses a smarter, simpler method: we leverage the notification emails already sitting in your Gmail.

This solution is entirely self-contained within Google’s free services (Sheets + Gmail).

The Automated Workflow (Set It and Forget It)

The script executes this highly efficient four-step workflow automatically:

  1. Search & Retrieve: It uses the powerful built-in Gmail service to search for all new emails matching the specific YouTube notification subject line.
  2. Extract the Data: The script quickly scans the raw email content. It uses clever data extraction techniques (Regular Expressions) to pull out the most important details: the subscriber’s name and their channel link.
  3. Log & Archive: The extracted Date, Subscriber Name, and Attribution Link are then logged as new entries in your Google Sheet.
  4. Clean Up: Once the data is safely logged, the script moves the corresponding email to the Trash, ensuring the same notification is never processed twice and achieving instant inbox decluttering.

Why This System Changes the Game

This system is built for efficiency and community engagement:

  • Declutter Your Gmail Inbox: For multi-channel creators, the deluge of notifications is a nightmare. This system processes and archives them automatically, reserving your inbox for real conversations.
  • Zero Hosting Cost: It runs inside Google’s free infrastructure, meaning no server bills or maintenance—ever.
  • A “Set It and Forget It” System: After the initial 15-minute setup, you set a time-driven trigger in the Apps Script dashboard. The script logs your new public subscribers while you sleep. No manual execution needed.

The Elegant Output: Your New Roster

We skip the raw email data and deliver a clean, actionable log focused on building relationships:

1. The Date 📅

Actionable Insight: See precisely when growth happened to correlate it with specific content or external events.

2. The Subscriber (Hyperlinked) 🔗

The One-Click Engagement Hack: The script uses a sheet formula to combine the name and the channel link (=HYPERLINK(...)). This gives you a one-click engagement tool: Click the name, land on their channel, and leave a thank-you comment. You just turned a passive subscriber into a loyal superfan.

3. Subscribed Channel 📺

Multi-Channel Utility: If you manage multiple channels (e.g., a main channel and a clips channel), this provides instant clarity on which asset is driving the growth—all aggregated in one central log.

Wrap-Up: Start Building Your Community Today

Stop letting the most valuable names in your community disappear into your email trash. This free, open-source script gives you the perfect tool to nurture your early supporters, spot collaboration opportunities, and keep your inbox clean.

👉 Get the Script on GitHub

If you found this tool useful, give the repo a Star ⭐️ on GitHub. It helps other creators find this free solution!

item.title: Supercharge Your Learning: A Free Userscript to Track Google Skills Progress
item.description:
Looking for an older version?

This post is for v3.0 and later, which supports the new Google Cloud Skills Boost platform. If you are looking for the archived post for the old Qwiklabs platform (v2.x), you can find it here: Userscript for Qwiklabs v2.0 (Archived).

Important Update

Google has renamed “Google Cloud Skills Boost” to “Google Skills” and moved its domain from cloudskillsboost.google to skills.google since October 2025. Please update the Lab Tracker userscript to v3.1.0 or later to ensure compatibility with the new domain. For more details on the change, see this related post from Google’s Blog.

Google Skills (formerly Qwiklabs) is a fantastic platform for gaining hands-on experience with Google Cloud. With hundreds of labs and courses, it’s a go-to resource for anyone serious about advancing their cloud skills.

But if you’re an active user like me, you’ve probably faced a common challenge: it’s hard to remember which labs you’ve already finished. To solve this, I created the Qwiklabs Lab Completion Tracker, a free userscript designed to streamline your learning journey.

This simple script runs in your browser with an extension like Tampermonkey (available for Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and others). It enhances the Google Skills website by adding clear visual indicators, so you can see your progress at a glance.

🚀 Key Features

  • Completion Markers: Automatically adds a green checkmark () next to completed labs and courses, so you never have to guess what you’ve already done.
  • “New” Badges: Instantly spot new labs and courses with an orange “New” badge () that have been added since your last sync.
  • One-Click Sync: Manually trigger a database update with the sync button () on your Progress page.

📸 Screenshots

The script adds helpful visual cues across the Google Skills website.

Sync and Manage Your Data

Managing your progress is simple. Just visit your Progress page and use the one-click sync button to update your local database with your latest completions.

Changelog

For a detailed list of updates and new features, check out the Version History page.

Future Plans

This script is actively maintained, and I’m always looking for ways to improve it. You can follow the project’s progress, report bugs, or suggest new features on the GitHub Issues page.

See Also:

  • Learning Google Cloud Platform on Qwiklabs: Learning Map, Assistive Tool and Tips

item.title: Qwiklabs Lab Completion Tracker - Version History
item.description:
This page provides a detailed version history for the Qwiklabs Lab Completion Tracker userscript. It documents the evolution of the script, from its initial release to the latest updates, detailing new features, improvements, and bug fixes designed to help users track their learning progress on Google Cloud Skills Boost (formerly Qwiklabs).

Back to the main post

v3.0.0 - Migration to Google Cloud Skills Boost 🚀

2025-10-04

This major release marks a significant milestone, migrating the entire script to support the platform’s rebranding from qwiklabs.com to the new Google Cloud Skills Boost (cloudskillsboost.google). This was a critical update to ensure continued functionality for all users. Beyond the migration, this version introduced several key enhancements. The script now automatically creates records for new activities, improving tracking on catalog search results. Under the hood, significant refactoring was done to improve robustness, readability, and security by avoiding innerHTML and centralizing ID extraction.

  • Breaking Change: Full migration from qwiklabs.com to cloudskillsboost.google.
  • Features:
    • Automatically create records for unregistered activities.
    • Improve activity tracking for catalog search results.
  • Fixes:
    • Improve robustness with null checks for DOM elements.
    • Improve main function’s error logging.
  • Refactoring:
    • Improve script robustness, readability, and security by avoiding innerHTML.
    • Centralize ID extraction using named groups in the router.

v2.1.0 - Enhanced Profile Page and Routing

2021-10-14

This release enhanced the profile page with several key features. Pagination was added to allow browsing of the entire activity history. Visual cues, such as warning icons for unrecognized labs and a red background for failed ones, were introduced to make it easier to identify items requiring attention.

The main function was also refactored to use a more robust routing system, improving code maintainability and structure.

v2.0.7 - Styling and Bug Fixes

2021-10-13

This maintenance release included styling adjustments to align with a new activity card design on Qwiklabs. It also addressed several bugs related to the database update feature to improve its reliability.

v2.0 - Revamp for New Qwiklabs Web Design 🚁

2021-09-30

Following a major platform redesign by Qwiklabs in late September 2021, this version was released with a significant code rewrite to ensure compatibility with the new user interface. This update restored all core features of the tracker.

v1.0 - Fix the Change in My Learning URL

2021-09-18

  • Replaced /my_learning with /profile/activity.
  • Changed the CSS selector and Regular Expression to look up the total number of activities.
  • Do not support pages with a URL starting with /my_learning.

v0.5.5 - Improved Activity Name Recognition

2021-05-04

To address parsing issues with activity names containing special characters, this release improved data integrity by using the unique lab/quest ID as the primary key for database updates, rather than relying on the activity name.

v0.5.4 - Fix the Change in My Learning Layout

2021-04-09

  • Added a quick link to view all My Learning Activity results
  • Revised the way to update the database records with the new format of learning activity data

v0.5.2 & v0.5.3 - Adapting to UI Changes

These minor updates were released to maintain compatibility with Qwiklabs’ evolving design. They included the removal of code for deprecated pages and adjustments to correctly parse the new Catalog and My Learning Activity table layouts.

v0.5.1

2020-06-14

A one-click sync button was introduced to improve user experience. This feature allows for an instant update of all lab and quest statuses in the local database, eliminating the need for manual checks.

v0.5.0

This foundational update migrated data storage from a simple markup-based system to IndexedDB, resulting in more efficient and scalable data handling. A significant portion of the codebase was also refactored to use modern async/await syntax, improving readability and maintainability.

For v0.4.8 or Earlier

Early versions of the script relied on a manually maintained list of completed labs stored within the userscript. The primary feature was a green check-circle () added to completed items on catalog pages to simplify progress tracking.

v0.4.8 - Support Tracking “Home” and “My Learning” Pages

2020-06-06

Tracking support was extended to the Home and My Learning pages. This version also introduced NEW badges and yellow highlights for activities not yet present in the database, making it easier to identify new content.

v0.4.5

2020-05-20

This version introduced visual highlights for completed items to improve visibility. Annotations were extended to the “Your Favorites” section, and colored backgrounds were added to tables on the “Completed Courses” and “Completed Labs” pages for easier scanning. To help distinguish between different activity types, a purple highlight was added for game records.

item.title: Securely Self-Host n8n with Docker, Traefik, and Cloudflare
item.description:
I’m a huge fan of n8n, the powerful workflow automation tool. While self-hosting n8n offers great flexibility, I found the initial setup process could be complex and time-consuming. I wanted a simple, secure, and repeatable way to deploy my own n8n instance. That’s why I created n8n-stack.

I’m excited to share it with you today!

Skip table of contents
Table of Contents
  • What is n8n-stack?
  • Why Self-Host n8n with This Stack?
  • Key Features of n8n-stack
  • Getting Started with Your Secure n8n Instance
  • Let Me Know What You Think
  • Take Control of Your Automation
  • References

What is n8n-stack?

n8n-stack (officially n8n-traefik-cloudflared-stack) is a production-ready set of configuration files to help you self-host n8n using Docker or Podman. It comes with Traefik as a reverse proxy and is secured by Cloudflare Zero Trust, giving you a private and secure n8n environment.

The project is based on an excellent tutorial by Kjetil Furas, but I’ve added my own tweaks and enhancements to make it even more robust and user-friendly for a seamless n8n self-hosting experience.

The stack includes:

  • n8n: The star of the show for all your automation needs.
  • Traefik: A modern reverse proxy to handle web traffic efficiently.
  • Cloudflare Tunnel: Securely exposes your n8n instance to the internet without opening firewall ports, thanks to Cloudflare Zero Trust.

Why Self-Host n8n with This Stack?

  • Enhanced Security: By using Cloudflare Tunnel, you don’t expose your server directly to the internet. All traffic is routed through Cloudflare’s secure network.
  • Simplified Management: The docker-compose.yml files are pre-configured for a quick start.
  • Cost-Effective: Self-hosting can be cheaper than paid plans, especially if you already have a server.
  • Full Control: You have complete control over your data, workflows, and the n8n environment.

Key Features of n8n-stack

  • Docker and Podman Ready: Includes compose files for both Docker and rootless Podman, so you can use your preferred container engine.
  • Security Hardened: The default configuration is enhanced for better security, such as using a file-based provider for Traefik.
  • Simple Backup and Restore: A handy script is included to help you back up and restore your n8n data with ease.
  • Clear Documentation: Comprehensive documentation is available in the /docs directory to guide you through every step.
  • Pinned Versions: n8n and traefik image versions are pinned to ensure stable and predictable deployments.

Getting Started with Your Secure n8n Instance

I’ve tried to make it as simple as possible to get up and running:

  1. Clone the repository:

     git clone https://github.com/chriskyfung/n8n-traefik-cloudflared-stack.git
 cd n8n-traefik-cloudflared-stack
  2. Follow the setup instructions: The README.md contains all the details for the initial configuration.
  3. Choose your container engine: Head over to the /docs directory and follow the specific guide for either Docker or Podman.

Let Me Know What You Think

I built n8n-stack to solve my own n8n self-hosting challenges, and I hope it can be useful to others in the community. I’d love for you to check it out on GitHub, give it a try, and share your feedback.

Check out the n8n-stack repository on GitHub

I’m always open to feedback and contributions, so feel free to open an issue or a pull request!

Take Control of Your Automation

Embarking on the self-hosting journey for your workflow automation can seem complex, but it doesn’t have to be a daunting task. With n8n-stack, you are equipped with a clear, secure, and production-ready path to unlock the full potential of n8n on your own terms. This project was born from the desire to eliminate the initial setup hurdles, providing a robust environment with Docker, Traefik, and Cloudflare right out of the box.

This stack is more than just a collection of configuration files; it’s a launchpad designed to get you up and running quickly, so you can focus on what truly matters: building powerful, creative automations that solve real-world problems. The power to innovate is now firmly in your hands. Dive in, explore the possibilities, and start your n8n journey today.

Happy Automating! 🚀

References

  • Self-Host n8n with Cloudflare Zero Trust and Docker by Kjetil Furas
  • n8n Documentation
  • Traefik Documentation
  • Cloudflare Zero Trust Documentation
  • Podman Documentation
  • Docker Documentation

item.title: InstapaperScraper: Export Your Bookmarks to CSV Effortlessly
item.description:
IMPORTANT

This post describes an early version of InstapaperScraper. The project has since been updated to v1.1, which offers more features, and is much more reliable.

Please read the new announcement post for more information.

In today’s digital age, managing saved Instapaper articles can be overwhelming, especially for free plan users who lack a search feature. Obtaining API keys for Instapaper APIs can also be time-consuming.

Say hello to InstapaperScraper—a lightweight Python script designed to simplify the process. With InstapaperScraper, you can scrape bookmarks from your Instapaper account and export them as neatly formatted CSV data—all without needing an API key.

Skip table of contents
Table of Contents
  • What Is InstapaperScraper?
  • Getting Started
  • Unlocking Data-Driven Insights
  • Contribute & Stay Updated

What Is InstapaperScraper?

InstapaperScraper is a simple Python tool for retrieving and organizing Instapaper bookmarks. Key features include:

  • Comprehensive Bookmark Scraping: Efficiently organize bookmarks from your homepage or specific folders, with pagination support to ensure no bookmarks are missed.

  • CSV Export: InstapaperScraper formats bookmark metadata—page number, ID, title, and URL—into CSV output, making it easy to import and analyze in tools like Excel or Google Sheets.

  • Effortless Configuration: Set up a .env file with your Instapaper credentials and optional folder settings to start scraping immediately.

Getting Started

Setting up InstapaperScraper is quick and straightforward. Follow these steps:

  1. Clone the Repository Download the project from GitHub.

  2. Install Dependencies Use the provided requirements.txt to install all necessary Python libraries:

    pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Configure Your Environment Create a .env file in the project root with the following:

    INSTAPAPER_USERNAME=your_username
INSTAPAPER_PASSWORD=your_password
ENABLE_FOLDER_MODE=true  # Optional: enable this to scrape a specific folder
FOLDER_ID_AND_SLUG=your_folder_id/your_folder_slug  # Required if folder mode is enabled

  4. Run the Scraper Export your bookmarks to CSV by running:

  python scrape.py > bookmarks.csv

With this setup, you can quickly back up your Instapaper bookmarks locally.

Unlocking Data-Driven Insights

The CSV output from InstapaperScraper opens up exciting possibilities for data analysis and visualization:

  • Filter by Page Index: Use text editors or Microsoft Excel to find the page number or bookmark ID of a specific bookmark, enabling you to locate its pagination or full-text view.

  • Analyze Reading Patterns: Track your reading habits over time to identify trends in interests or productivity.

  • Categorize Bookmarks: Organize bookmarks by topic or source to gain insights into your preferred content types.

  • Set Goals and Automate Recommendations: Integrate bookmark data with other tools to create personalized reading goals, recommendations, or automated content curation.

Contribute & Stay Updated

InstapaperScraper is an open-source project on GitHub (chriskyfung/InstapaperScraper) that welcomes contributions. Share your ideas, join discussions, or submit pull requests to help advance the project.

Happy scraping, and may your bookmarks always be in order!

item.title: Fix Netlify CLI & Node Task List `ntl` Conflict on Windows with Volta
item.description:
If you’re a web developer hosting your projects on Netlify, chances are the Netlify CLI (netlify-cli) is one of your must-have tools for streamline deployments, configuring site settings, and debugging issues—all right from your terminal. At the same time, Node Task List (ntl) is a fantastic utlity that scans your package.json and creates an interactive menu of npm scripts. With NTL, you can easily launch tasks like starting a server or running builds in just a few clicks, saving you time and effort.

The catch is that both packages attempt to register the same executable name, ntl. When installed globally via npm, this results in a naming conflict and produces installation errors.

Common Errors

On Linux with NVM:

# Terminal

$ npm install -g ntl
npm error code EEXIST
npm error path /home/user/.nvm/versions/node/v20.17.0/bin/ntl
npm error EEXIST: file already exists
npm error File exists: /home/user/.nvm/versions/node/v20.17.0/bin/ntl
npm error Remove the existing file and try again, or run npm
npm error with --force to overwrite files recklessly.

Luckily, fixing the conflict on Linux is straightforward if you’re using the Node version manager NVM. Simply install netlify-cli first, then force the installation of ntl using the --force option to overwrite the conflicting executable.

On Windows with Volta:

# PowerShell

$ npm install -g ntl --force
Volta error: Executable 'ntl' is already installed by netlify-cli

Please remove netlify-cli before installing ntl

For Windows users especially who rely on Volta as their Node version manager, the issue is a bit trickier. Volta doesn’t allow conflicting executables, so forcing the installation of ntl won’t resolve the problem.

In this guide, we’ll walk you through the steps to resolve the naming conflict on Windows, ensuring that both tools can coexist peacefully and letting you enjoy the best of both worlds in your development workflow.

Step-by-Step Guide to Fix the Conflict

1. Uninstall Node Task List (if already installed)

To ensure there are no lingering conflicts, begin by uninstalling any global installation of Node Task List:

npm uninstall -g ntl

2. Install Netlify CLI

Next, install the Netlify CLI globally. This sets up the ntl executable for Netlify:

npm install -g netlify-cli

3. Verify the Netlify CLI Installation

Check that Netlify CLI is properly installed by running:

volta which ntl

You should see an output similar to:

C:\Users\<User Name>\AppData\Local\Volta\tools\image\packages\netlify-cli\ntl

4. Modify Volta’s User Binaries Configuration

Open File Explorer and navigate to:

C:\Users\<User Name>\AppData\Local\Volta\tools\user\bins\

5. Remove the Conflicting Registration File

In the bins folder, locate and delete the file named ntl.json. Removing this file clears the conflicting registration for the ntl command.

6. Reinstall Node Task List

With the conflicting file removed, reinstall Node Task List globally:

npm install -g ntl

This command registers Node Task List with its own executable path without interfering with Netlify CLI.

7. Verify Both Installations Are Working Separately

Finally, ensure both tools are correctly configured by checking their respective executable paths:

volta which ntl
volta which netlify

The outputs should point to different locations, for example:

  • Node Task List: ...\Volta\tools\image\packages\ntl\ntl
  • Netlify CLI: ...\Volta\tools\image\packages\netlify-cli\netlify

Now you’re all set! You can use the ntl command to run Node Task List tasks, while the netlify command will work with the Netlify CLI—without any conflicts.

Conclusion

By following these steps, you resolve the executable naming conflict between Node Task List and Netlify CLI on a Windows machine with Volta. This solution is perfect for Netlify developers who want to maintain a smooth, efficient workflow without installation hassles.

If you’re interested in more tips, be sure to check out our blog for additional resources.

Happy coding!

Additional Resources

  • Get started with Netlify CLI | Netlify Docs
  • Top 10 Netlify CLI Commands - DEV Community
